render_data.title: Heat transfer from a hot radiator
render_data.prompt: Match each heat-transfer process label to the scenario shown around the hot radiator.

bins.label: Warm air rising above the radiator
Heat felt from a distance without contact
Heat spreading through the metal bracket
bins.hint: heat through a solid in contact

image_prompt: SVG: heat-transfer scene around a hot radiator with draggable labels (Convection, Radiation, Conduction) and three numbered answer markers. Dashed orange convection arrows rise above the radiator (warm air rising); wavy red radiation arrows reach a person on the right; a blue conduction arrow runs along a metal bracket to the floor.

SVG: labelled cross-section of a thermos flask containing hot coffee. Leader-line labels: Plastic stopper, Double-walled metal container, Vacuum (no particles → no conduction/convection), Silvered-surface double-walled glass, Hot coffee, Insulated support. Small cyclist scene for context.

render_data.title: Build a helium atom
render_data.prompt: A helium atom has 2 protons, 2 neutrons and 2 electrons. Place each particle into the correct region of the atom.

image_prompt: SVG: blank atom-building canvas (dark) with dashed inner nucleus circle and dashed outer electron-shell circle, plus a draggable-items panel showing a red + (proton), white 0 (neutron) and blue − (electron). Build the helium atom (2p, 2n, 2e).

topic: Negatively charged toner particle and positively charged drum
image_prompt: SVG: a positively charged drum surface (row of red + charges along a curved metal drum) above a single negatively charged toner particle (blue circle with −) with a photosensitive paper below. Dashed answer region asks student to draw force arrows.

topic: Solar system (label asteroid belt, Mercury, Saturn)

render_data.title: Label the solar system
render_data.prompt: Place each label on the feature it identifies in the solar system diagram.

bins.label: Ring of rocky objects between Mars and Jupiter

image_prompt: SVG: solar system on a starfield — Sun at left, all eight planets along orbit arcs, asteroid belt band of dots between Mars and Jupiter, Saturn with rings. Three red arrows point UP at Mercury, the asteroid belt, and Saturn; draggable labels (Asteroid belt, Mercury, Saturn) sit beneath for the student to place.

render_data.title: Gravitational attraction on Apophis
render_data.caption: The Sun's gravitational force always points toward the Sun. The force is stronger at position B (closer) than at position A (farther), so the arrow at B is longer.

image_prompt: SVG: hyperbolic flyby trajectory of Apophis on starfield. Orange arrow path enters from upper-left, swings below the Sun, and exits lower-right. Position A marked far from the Sun (upper-left), position B closer to the Sun (lower middle). Earth shown for context near close approach. Student draws gravitational-force arrows.

topic: Parachute model in wind-tunnel air flow
image_prompt: SVG: parachute model with skydiver held in an upward air stream inside a wind tunnel. Two columns of upward blue air-flow arrows labelled 'Air flow'. Caption: 'Parachute model held in upward air stream — drag balances weight'.

render_data.title: Classify the experiment variables
render_data.prompt: Place each quantity into the box that best describes its role in the parachute experiment.

image_prompt: SVG: clean ruled table with shaded header row. Columns: Quantity | Independent variable (IV) | Dependent variable (DV) | Control variable (CV). Rows: Weight, Air speed, CSA, Mass, Volume — each with empty checkboxes in the three variable columns.

topic: Box of parachute models — CSA selection

render_data.title: Select the parachute models to use
render_data.prompt: From the box of parachute models (each labelled Weight / CSA), choose the selection that gives a valid experiment to relate weight to air speed. Weight must be controlled while CSA is varied across the widest possible range.

options.label: Five models that all support the SAME weight, with CSA values spread over the greatest possible range at regular intervals
Five models that all have the SAME CSA but different weights
Two models with the largest and smallest CSA only
All ten models regardless of their weights and CSAs

image_prompt: SVG: brown 'Available box' with ten red balls each labelled with weight and CSA (0.80N/10cm², 0.40N/12cm², 0.60N/9cm², 0.60N/12cm², 1.20N/12cm², 1.20N/12cm², 1.00N/12cm², 1.00N/11cm², 0.80N/12cm², 0.40N/8cm²), an empty blue 'Equipment box' with ten holes, and a key (Weight / CSA).

SVG: student's recorded raw data shown as a row of red balls annotated with size and measured air speed. First ball labelled Radius = 4.0 cm (not area!) → 14 m s⁻¹; remaining areas 13, 20, 24, 16, 28, 33 cm² with air speeds 25, 22, 19, 23, 17, 16 m s⁻¹.

SVG: two side panels of a log floating in water — lightly loaded sitting high, heavily loaded sitting low — captioned 'The log sinks lower in the water as its weight increases', above a clean ruled results table. Columns: Weight of the log / N | Volume of log below water / cm³; rows 0.5→50, 1.1→110, 2.0→200, 4.2→420.
